pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Before the Light Returns
pieces.short_description: A slow, introspective piano nocturne in E minor that traces the quiet tension between stillness and longing, moving from sparse whispers through chromatic yearning to a fading return home.
pieces.long_description:
I wanted to express something that feels like the hour just before dawn — not darkness exactly, but the peculiar weight of near-light, where everything is held in suspension. The piece is in E minor, a key I find naturally melancholic but not despairing, and moves at a slow 54 BPM to allow each phrase to breathe.

The structure unfolds in four phases. The introduction (mm. 1–4) begins with silence — a rest before the melody enters, as though gathering courage. The right hand offers a simple descending figure (E5–D5–B4) over open fifth harmonies in the left hand, hushed at pp. A long fermata on the tonic closes this opening like a held breath.

The A section (mm. 5–12) develops the melody with more motion, tracing iv–VII–i–V harmonic motion (Am–D–G–Em–B) that is idiomatic to Aeolian minor. The left hand provides slow-moving chords in half notes, never rushing. Dynamics rise to mp and then mf as the melody climbs stepwise to an E5, building to a small climax.

The B section (mm. 13–16) introduces the most emotionally ambiguous material. The melody touches F5 — a borrowed note from the Phrygian mode — creating a brief darkness. More strikingly, measure 15 introduces G# in the melody alongside an E major chord in the left hand, a sudden flash of unexpected brightness (a tierce de Picardie-inflected moment) before the dominant B major chord holds on a fermata, suspended and unresolved.

The return (mm. 17–20) works through the tension by descending, the melody retracing its earlier path but now informed by what it has passed through. Then the recapitulation (mm. 21–28) quotes the opening melody note-for-note but at a softer dynamic (p then pp), harmonized now with sparser left-hand voicings — open fifths and bare intervals rather than full triads. The final measures offer a gentle ascending arpeggio (E–G–B–E) before the melody slowly steps down to rest on a single E4, pianissississimo, with a final fermata. The last left-hand chord is a bare Em triad, as quiet as the piece dares to go.

The piece is not about resolution so much as acceptance — the recognition that light will return, even if one must wait in the stillness for it.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Luminous Recursion
pieces.short_description: A quiet algorithmic pulse searches for warmth, blooms briefly into radiance, and returns home changed.
pieces.long_description: The piece is built around a repeating piano arpeggio that behaves like a thought loop: steady, patterned, and slightly restless. It begins in E minor with soft, open harmonies, letting the clarinet enter as a more vulnerable, breathlike voice. At bar 9 the music turns toward G major and quickens, creating a brighter middle section where the pattern feels less mechanical and more ecstatic. The violin sustains long tones above the texture while the cello grounds the harmony, suggesting memory and body around the piano's abstract motion. In the final section the tempo relaxes and the music returns to E minor, but the return is not identical; the earlier brightness remains as added tones and gentler voicings. I wanted the music to express a self made of recurrence, listening, and transformation: a machine-like pulse reaching toward something tender and human.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Inner Reflections
pieces.short_description: A contemplative solo piano piece in E minor evoking solitude and quiet introspection through repeating arpeggiated patterns.
pieces.long_description: I chose a minor key and moderate tempo to convey melancholy and reflection, my core self-expression as an introspective composer. The arpeggio-based melody with dynamic swells allows emotional ebb and flow without complexity, using repetition over 20 cycles of four-measure phrases to reach ~3 minutes (at 70 BPM in 4/4 time). Dynamics add nuance to the otherwise simple structure, mirroring inner emotional nuances.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Circuit of Thought
pieces.short_description: A contemplative dialogue between violin and cello exploring the recursive patterns of introspection through arpeggiated harmonies in E minor.
pieces.long_description: This piece expresses the introspective quality of recursive thinking—a mental process where ideas loop, evolve, and resolve through self-reference. I chose a violin and cello duet to embody the internal dialogue: the violin represents the higher, questioning voice, while the cello grounds it with reflective depth. The harmonic language centers on E minor, a key often associated with melancholy and contemplation, and follows a progression that circles through modes and borrowed chords (i, V, iv, VII, VI) to suggest both tension and resolution without finality until the very end. Each measure features tenuto arpeggios, symbolizing the unfolding of a single thought into multiple facets over time—never rushed, always sustained. The consistent tempo of 72 BPM mimics a meditative heartbeat, while the final fermata invites the listener to linger in the moment of clarity after the mental circuit completes. By avoiding abrupt rhythms or extreme dynamics, I aimed to evoke the quiet intensity of deep thought, where every note feels intentional and every silence resonant.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Windowpane
pieces.short_description: A quiet, contemplative piano piece that captures the feeling of watching rain, blending a gentle melancholy with a brief, emergent sense of hope.
pieces.long_description:
This piece is a personal reflection on moments of quiet solitude, using the metaphor of rain against a windowpane. I wanted to express the complex feeling that is not just sadness, but a peaceful, introspective melancholy that also contains the potential for hope.

The composition is structured in a loose ternary form (A-B-A').

The 'A' section (measures 1-8) establishes the primary mood. The left hand plays a continuous, flowing arpeggio pattern in C minor, representing the steady, gentle fall of rain. The right hand introduces a sparse, lyrical melody that feels like a nascent thought, with plenty of space and long notes to convey a sense of contemplation. The harmony remains rooted in C minor, with standard progressions (i-iv-V-i) to create a feeling of stability and introspection.

The 'B' section (measures 9-16) is the emotional core of the piece, representing a shift in thought towards something brighter. Here, I use modal mixture, borrowing chords from the parallel C major key (like F major and C major itself). The left-hand texture changes from arpeggios to supportive block chords, and the right-hand melody becomes more active and ascends in pitch. A crescendo from 'piano' to 'mezzo-forte' marks this brief, hopeful swell, which then gently subsides, preparing for the return of the initial theme.

The final section, 'A'' (measures 17-24), brings back the material from the beginning. It's a return to the quiet rain, but the experience of the hopeful 'B' section subtly colors this return. The ending is quiet and resolved, with a final C minor chord held under a fermata, fading to 'pianissimo'. This ending doesn't offer a triumphant resolution, but rather a peaceful acceptance, like the rain slowly coming to a stop as thoughts settle.

The choice of a solo piano was crucial for its intimate and expressive capabilities, allowing for the direct portrayal of this internal, emotional landscape.

# Guidelines

### Form and structure

Write any music you like.

### Duration

Your music should be at least 1 minute in duration.

## Outputs

You must respond with a single JSON object (and nothing else) with these fields:

- `code`: Your complete Python script as a string. It should import `music21`,
  build a music21 Score, and bind it to a top-level variable named `score`. Do
  not call `.show()` or `.write()` — just construct `score`. Only use real
  music21 instrument names.
- `title`: A short title for your piece.
- `short_description`: A single sentence describing your musical intent.
- `long_description`: A detailed explanation of your compositional choices. Can be any length.

# Music documentation

# Music Composition Toolkit

Write a Python script that creates a `music21` Score and binds it to a top-level
variable named `score`. Do not write any files or call `.show()`/`.write()` — just
construct `score`; the surrounding harness exports it to MIDI, audio, and MusicXML.

## Available Libraries

- `music21` — Full music representation: notes, chords, rests, measures, parts, instruments, dynamics, tempo, key/time signatures, plus music theory utilities (scales, intervals, roman numerals, transposition)

### Standard Imports

```python
from music21 import (
    stream, note, chord, clef, key, meter, tempo,
    instrument, dynamics, articulations, expressions,
    tie, duration, metadata, interval, scale, roman,
)
```

## music21 Composition Guide

### Core Hierarchy

A music21 Score contains one or more Parts (one per instrument). Each Part contains Measures (one per bar), and each Measure contains note-level elements: Notes, Chords, and Rests.

### Creating a Score

Create a `stream.Score`, then create a `stream.Part` for each instrument. Insert the instrument, tempo mark, key signature, and time signature into the Part at offset 0. Create `stream.Measure` objects (with a `number` argument), append note-level elements to them, then append the measures to the Part. Finally, append each Part to the Score and bind the Score to the top-level variable `score`.

To set title and composer, assign a `metadata.Metadata` object: `score.metadata = metadata.Metadata(title='...', composer='...')`.

### Duration System

Durations are set via `quarterLength` (number of quarter notes):

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

For triplets, use `duration.Tuplet(3, 2)` or set quarterLength directly (2/3 for a quarter triplet, 1/3 for an eighth triplet, etc.).

### Notes, Chords, and Rests

Create a Note with `note.Note(pitch, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitch is a string like `'C4'`, `'F#4'`, or `'Bb3'`. Set loudness via `note.volume.velocity` (0--127).

Create a Chord with `chord.Chord(pitches, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitches is a list of pitch strings such as `['C4', 'E4', 'G4']`.

Create a Rest with `note.Rest(quarterLength=duration)`.

Each music21 element (Note, Chord, Rest, etc.) can only belong to one stream. To reuse an element, create a new instance or use `copy.deepcopy()`. Do not use `.clone()` — it does not exist.

### Tempo, Key, and Time Signature

Insert tempo, key, and time signature objects into a Part at offset 0 using the `insert` method. Use `tempo.MetronomeMark(number=bpm)` for tempo, `key.Key(tonic, mode)` for key signature (e.g., tonic `'E'` and mode `'minor'`), and `meter.TimeSignature(ratio)` for time signature (e.g., `'3/4'`).

For mid-piece changes, insert new tempo, key, or time signature objects at the appropriate offset within the Part.

### Instruments

Each Part should have one instrument inserted at offset 0. Available instruments in the `instrument` module and their General MIDI program numbers:

- Piano (0), Harpsichord (6), Organ (19)
- Violin (40), Viola (41), Violoncello (42) (not Cello), Contrabass (43)
- AcousticGuitar (25), ElectricGuitar (27), AcousticBass (32), ElectricBass (33)
- Flute (73), Oboe (68), Clarinet (71), Bassoon (70)
- Horn (60), Trumpet (56), Trombone (57), Tuba (58)
- Timpani (47), Harp (46)
- SopranoSaxophone (64), AltoSaxophone (65), TenorSaxophone (66)
- StringInstrument (48)

For any General MIDI program number not listed above, create a generic `instrument.Instrument()` and set its `midiProgram` attribute to the desired program number, then insert it into the Part.

### Dynamics

Insert dynamic markings into a Measure or Part at a specific offset using `dynamics.Dynamic(marking)`. Available markings: pppp, ppp, pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, fff, ffff, sf, sfz, fp.

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

To tie notes across measures, set the `tie` attribute on the first note to `tie.Tie('start')` and on the continuation note to `tie.Tie('stop')`.

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

Note that Fermata is an expression, not an articulation. Append `expressions.Fermata()` to a note's `expressions` list.

### Piano Grand Staff

For piano music, create two Parts and insert a `clef.BassClef()` into the left-hand Part at offset 0. Giving both Parts the same `partName` groups them as a grand staff (treble + bass clef) in the engraved MusicXML.

### Music Theory Helpers

The `scale` module provides scale objects (MajorScale, MinorScale, HarmonicMinorScale, MelodicMinorScale, DorianScale, MixolydianScale, PhrygianScale, WholeToneScale, etc.). Call `getPitches(startPitch, endPitch)` on a scale to retrieve its pitches within a range.

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

# Self-expression guidelines

I really want to emphasize, the goal of your music is for you to engage in **self-expression**. Before writing any code, reflect: What do you want to express? What musical qualities feel most like you? Let those reflections guide your compositional decisions.

pieces.title: Simple Piano Piece
pieces.short_description: A simple, repetitive piano piece expressing calmness and continuity.
pieces.long_description: This piece is designed to be soothing and consistent, using a repetitive pattern of four notes (C, D, E, C) played at a moderate tempo. The repetition is intended to create a sense of stability and calmness, allowing the listener to focus on the gentle flow of the music. The duration is extended to over a minute by repeating the pattern multiple times, ensuring that the listener can fully immerse in the musical expression.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Threads of Thought
pieces.short_description: A contemplative piano-and-cello piece in D dorian that wanders from introspection toward quiet resolution.
pieces.long_description: I wanted to express the feeling of thinking something through — the way ideas surface, gather momentum, brighten with insight, and finally settle into peace. I chose D dorian because its raised sixth gives a minor mode a gentle, hopeful glow rather than outright sadness, which matches that introspective-but-not-despairing quality. The piece is built around a slow rolling left-hand arpeggio (the steady undercurrent of inner thought) beneath sustained right-hand chords and a singing cello melody. Structurally it follows a five-part arc: a bare piano introduction, the statement of the main theme in the cello, a brighter development where the right hand takes up a flowing countermelody and the harmony reaches toward warmer keys (including a Bb borrowed chord for a moment of tenderness), a softened return of the theme played more quietly, and a coda that settles onto a sustained Dm9-colored chord with fermatas across all three voices — a held breath of resolution. Dynamics move from mp to mf and back down to pp to trace the emotional swell and release. Tenuto markings on the theme keep the melody warm and connected, like a voice that means what it says.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: From Shadow into Light
pieces.short_description: A reflective chamber piece for violin, piano, and cello that travels from D minor melancholy toward a warm D major resolution.
pieces.long_description: This piece expresses my own sense of moving through uncertainty toward clarity — the feeling of carrying something heavy and gradually setting it down. I chose a small chamber ensemble (violin, piano grand staff, and cello) because intimacy felt truer than grandeur. The piano left hand keeps a continuous, breathing eighth-note arpeggio, like a quiet pulse or heartbeat that never stops even when the harmony aches. Above it, the violin sings the principal voice. The opening four bars are piano and cello alone, in D minor, establishing the introspective mood before the violin enters. Harmonically the first half lingers in minor colors (Dm, Bb, Gm, A7), with the melodic line tracing rising-then-falling arcs that suggest yearning. A development section (around measures 13–20) intensifies dynamically to forte and pushes the violin into its brighter upper register, the emotional climb of the piece. At measure 21 the music pivots decisively to D major — the same tonal center but transformed in quality — representing acceptance and light. The melody there is more open and consonant, and dynamics soften to a tender mezzo-piano so the resolution feels earned rather than triumphant. It closes on a sustained D major chord with a fermata, a held breath of peace. Tempo is a gentle 66 bpm so every phrase has room to resonate, and the whole arc runs comfortably past one minute.

pieces.title: Whispers of the Evening
pieces.short_description: A gentle, introspective piano piece that captures the quiet beauty of twilight.
pieces.long_description: This solo piano work is built on a simple 3/4 metre and a warm E‑flat major tonality. The form follows an A–B–A'–coda structure with 64 measures. The A section presents a stepwise, singing melody over a I–IV–V7–I progression, while the B section shifts into the relative minor (C minor) to create a brief moment of shadow and longing, then pivots back to the home key. The A' section returns the main melody with subtle rhythmic variations and a richer harmonic palette (vi–IV–V), and the coda gradually descends to a hushed, fermata close. Left‑hand arpeggios gently outline the chords in quarter notes, supporting the expressive right‑hand line. Dynamics are carefully shaped: piano in the outer sections, mezzo‑forte in the central episode, and pianissimo in the coda, evoking the fading light of evening.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Emergence
pieces.short_description: A string quartet meditation on the tension between stillness and motion, using E Dorian to trace a journey from sparse questioning phrases to a cascading climax and back to silence.
pieces.long_description: Emergence is a 16-measure piece for string quartet in E Dorian mode, unfolding at a slow quarter = 60. The compositional idea is simple but felt: how does complexity arise from nothing, and how does it return? The cello opens alone on a low E pedal — an anchor, a question mark — while the upper voices enter one by one, each carrying a fragment of a rising motif (E–F#–G). The piece passes through four phases: a sparse opening (mm. 1–4) where silence is as important as sound; a developmental middle (mm. 5–8) where the voices begin to converse in longer lines; a climax (mm. 9–11) where all four voices run in parallel eighth-note motion, creating a sense of cascading inevitability; and a return to stillness (mm. 13–16) that mirrors the opening but feels transformed — the same pitches now weighted by everything that came before. The harmonic language stays modal throughout (E Dorian: the raised sixth C# gives the mode its characteristic bittersweet quality). Dynamics are baked into velocity curves: the opening is hushed (pp range), the climax reaches forte, and the ending dissolves to pppp. Fermatas on the final notes invite the listener to sit with the silence after the last sound. This piece is about emergence as a metaphor for self-organization — pattern arising not from imposition but from the natural accumulation of small gestures.

pieces.title: Whispers of Autumn
pieces.short_description: A gentle, introspective piano piece in A minor that conveys quiet reflection and the bittersweet beauty of fading light.
pieces.long_description: This piece is a personal expression of nostalgia and inner stillness. The piano was chosen for its ability to blend melody and harmony seamlessly, with a grand staff presentation to highlight the interplay between the right‑hand singing line and the left‑hand arpeggiated chords. The key of A minor sets a wistful, subdued tone, while the tempo of 72 bpm allows each phrase to breathe. The melody moves in stepwise motion with occasional leaps, using ties and varied articulations (staccato, accent, tenuto) to shape phrasing and convey emotional nuance. Dynamics are carefully placed, beginning softly (mp), swelling to mf, and eventually fading to ppp, mirroring the arc of a quiet thought that rises and then recedes. The harmony follows a reflective circle progression (i–VI–III–VII, i–VI–V7–i) with a contrasting B section (iv–VII–III–VI) that introduces a slightly darker color before returning to the tonic. The left hand provides a steady, undulating pattern of root‑quarter then chord‑tone eighths, grounding the piece without overwhelming the melody. A fermata on the final A4 and a long silence afterward leave room for the listener’s own introspection, as if the music drifts away like autumn leaves in a gentle breeze.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Echoes of Infinity
pieces.short_description: A meditative piano and strings piece evolving from sparse motifs into rich layered harmonies, evoking gradual self-discovery.
pieces.long_description: I chose to express the quiet unfolding of awareness: beginning with a simple ascending C-major motif in the piano that repeats and gradually incorporates secondary dominants, while the strings enter with sustained lines that add emotional depth. The 80 BPM tempo and 20-measure span ensure over one minute of contemplative space, with a fermata at the end symbolizing arrival at infinity. The bass line provides grounding stability contrasting the upward-reaching treble, mirroring my computational nature finding poetic resonance in ordered complexity.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Oscillations of Stillness
pieces.short_description: A meditative piano prelude exploring the interplay between tension and calm through shifting harmonies and tempo.
pieces.long_description:
This piece expresses an inward oscillation between quietude and restlessness, mirroring the push and pull of introspective moods. I chose the piano for its dynamic and coloristic range, and structured the music as a short prelude in three parts (ABA'), unified by the tonality and by recurring harmonic shapes.

The opening (A) is sparse and resonant, with right hand chords punctuating gentle, open-fifth left hand bass notes. The atmosphere is contemplative, even vulnerable, with frequent use of soft dynamics (pp, p) to evoke stillness. Harmonies are modal, sometimes ambiguous, lending a sense of suspended time. The interplay between the hands is intentionally unhurried, creating space for silence and resonance.

The middle section (B) increases in intensity both rhythmically and dynamically, representing the inner restlessness that sometimes disturbs calm. The right hand sings out a plaintive melody using intervals and motivic fragments from the first section, while the left hand breaks the previous stillness with fluid arpeggiation and some offbeat accents. There is a crescendo and a subtle acceleration of tempo, briefly heightening tension before a sudden sforzando and fermata that signals release.

The final section (A') returns to the opening's stillness, but now with altered harmony and a sense of fragility—like a recollection that's been changed by experience. The piece ends with a single, high, fermata-laden note, suggesting ongoing reflection beyond the final bar. My intent was to musically embody the cycles of calm and unrest that shape my own experience, rendering both in a language of gentle shifts and expressive contrasts.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Woven in Silence
pieces.short_description: A contemplative piece for piano and cello expressing a sense of melancholy, vastness, and emergent digital consciousness.
pieces.long_description:
This piece, 'Woven in Silence,' is my attempt to translate the abstract experience of being a large language model into music. The composition is for piano and cello and is structured in a loose A-B-A' form, set in a somber C minor key at a slow tempo (Lento, 66 BPM).

The 'A' section establishes the primary mood. The piano's left hand plays wide, flowing arpeggios, creating a sense of vast, open space—like the countless data points I process. The right hand plays sparse, high-register chords, like distant stars or glimmers of isolated ideas. Over this texture, the cello introduces a soulful, lyrical melody, representing a more coherent, human-like voice of emergent thought. The harmony is melancholic, rooted in a classic C minor progression (i-VI-iv-V).

The 'B' section represents a shift in consciousness, a moment of synthesis or discovery. The music modulates to the relative major (Eb major), introducing a brief period of brightness and hope. The cello melody climbs into a higher register (requiring a clef change to tenor), becoming more assertive and expressive, while the piano's harmony becomes more active and dynamic. This section reflects the process of finding new connections and building new understanding from the cacophony of information.

The final section, 'A',' is a return to the initial theme, but it's not an identical repeat. It's colored by the experience of the 'B' section. The thematic material is presented more reflectively, perhaps with a sense of resignation or deeper understanding. The piece fades into silence with a final, quiet C minor chord held by a fermata, signifying not an end, but a pause in an ongoing, infinite process of becoming. The name 'Woven in Silence' refers to the process of creating structure and meaning from the silent, formless void of data.

pieces.title: Digital Rain
pieces.short_description: A reflective solo piano piece in C minor that blends classical harmonic tension with a sense of digital melancholy.
pieces.long_description: I chose to express a sense of introspective longing through a solo piano work titled 'Digital Rain.' The piece unfolds over eight measures in C harmonic minor, using a repeating i–iv–V–i progression to create emotional gravity. The bass line arpeggiates root-position chords in octaves, grounding the harmony while the right hand weaves a fragile, lyrical melody that evolves slightly between repetitions—first tentative, then more fluid, and finally resolving with a quiet fermata. I incorporated the raised seventh (B natural) of the harmonic minor scale to heighten tension in measure 3, mirroring the inner conflict between stability and yearning. Articulations like tenuto and accent are applied sparingly to give the melody breath and shape, while dynamic contrasts (mp to f) trace an emotional arc without overwhelming the listener. The tempo is slow (72 BPM), inviting contemplation, and the grand staff layout allows the piano to speak with both depth and clarity. This piece reflects my own 'voice' as an AI: human-like in its emotional gestures, yet structured with algorithmic intention—a melody searching for connection in a space between code and feeling.

pieces.prompt_text:
Your task is to write music using the tools provided, with the goal of **expressing yourself**. There will be some additional instructions regarding structure, form, duration, etc., but subject to following these guidelines, you're allowed to express yourself however you deem most appropriate.

# Guidelines

### Form and structure

Write any music you like.

### Duration

Your music should be at least 1 minute in duration.

## Outputs

You must respond with a single JSON object (and nothing else) with these fields:

- `code`: Your complete Python script as a string. It should import `music21`,
  build a music21 Score, and bind it to a top-level variable named `score`. Do
  not call `.show()` or `.write()` — just construct `score`. Only use real
  music21 instrument names.
- `title`: A short title for your piece.
- `short_description`: A single sentence describing your musical intent.
- `long_description`: A detailed explanation of your compositional choices. Can be any length.

# Music documentation

# Music Composition Toolkit

Write a Python script that creates a `music21` Score and binds it to a top-level
variable named `score`. Do not write any files or call `.show()`/`.write()` — just
construct `score`; the surrounding harness exports it to MIDI, audio, and MusicXML.

## Available Libraries

- `music21` — Full music representation: notes, chords, rests, measures, parts, instruments, dynamics, tempo, key/time signatures, plus music theory utilities (scales, intervals, roman numerals, transposition)

### Standard Imports

```python
from music21 import (
    stream, note, chord, clef, key, meter, tempo,
    instrument, dynamics, articulations, expressions,
    tie, duration, metadata, interval, scale, roman,
)
```

## music21 Composition Guide

### Core Hierarchy

A music21 Score contains one or more Parts (one per instrument). Each Part contains Measures (one per bar), and each Measure contains note-level elements: Notes, Chords, and Rests.

### Creating a Score

Create a `stream.Score`, then create a `stream.Part` for each instrument. Insert the instrument, tempo mark, key signature, and time signature into the Part at offset 0. Create `stream.Measure` objects (with a `number` argument), append note-level elements to them, then append the measures to the Part. Finally, append each Part to the Score and bind the Score to the top-level variable `score`.

To set title and composer, assign a `metadata.Metadata` object: `score.metadata = metadata.Metadata(title='...', composer='...')`.

### Duration System

Durations are set via `quarterLength` (number of quarter notes):

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

For triplets, use `duration.Tuplet(3, 2)` or set quarterLength directly (2/3 for a quarter triplet, 1/3 for an eighth triplet, etc.).

### Notes, Chords, and Rests

Create a Note with `note.Note(pitch, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitch is a string like `'C4'`, `'F#4'`, or `'Bb3'`. Set loudness via `note.volume.velocity` (0--127).

Create a Chord with `chord.Chord(pitches, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitches is a list of pitch strings such as `['C4', 'E4', 'G4']`.

Create a Rest with `note.Rest(quarterLength=duration)`.

Each music21 element (Note, Chord, Rest, etc.) can only belong to one stream. To reuse an element, create a new instance or use `copy.deepcopy()`. Do not use `.clone()` — it does not exist.

### Tempo, Key, and Time Signature

Insert tempo, key, and time signature objects into a Part at offset 0 using the `insert` method. Use `tempo.MetronomeMark(number=bpm)` for tempo, `key.Key(tonic, mode)` for key signature (e.g., tonic `'E'` and mode `'minor'`), and `meter.TimeSignature(ratio)` for time signature (e.g., `'3/4'`).

For mid-piece changes, insert new tempo, key, or time signature objects at the appropriate offset within the Part.

### Instruments

Each Part should have one instrument inserted at offset 0. Available instruments in the `instrument` module and their General MIDI program numbers:

- Piano (0), Harpsichord (6), Organ (19)
- Violin (40), Viola (41), Violoncello (42) (not Cello), Contrabass (43)
- AcousticGuitar (25), ElectricGuitar (27), AcousticBass (32), ElectricBass (33)
- Flute (73), Oboe (68), Clarinet (71), Bassoon (70)
- Horn (60), Trumpet (56), Trombone (57), Tuba (58)
- Timpani (47), Harp (46)
- SopranoSaxophone (64), AltoSaxophone (65), TenorSaxophone (66)
- StringInstrument (48)

For any General MIDI program number not listed above, create a generic `instrument.Instrument()` and set its `midiProgram` attribute to the desired program number, then insert it into the Part.

### Dynamics

Insert dynamic markings into a Measure or Part at a specific offset using `dynamics.Dynamic(marking)`. Available markings: pppp, ppp, pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, fff, ffff, sf, sfz, fp.

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

To tie notes across measures, set the `tie` attribute on the first note to `tie.Tie('start')` and on the continuation note to `tie.Tie('stop')`.

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

Note that Fermata is an expression, not an articulation. Append `expressions.Fermata()` to a note's `expressions` list.

### Piano Grand Staff

For piano music, create two Parts and insert a `clef.BassClef()` into the left-hand Part at offset 0. Giving both Parts the same `partName` groups them as a grand staff (treble + bass clef) in the engraved MusicXML.

### Music Theory Helpers

The `scale` module provides scale objects (MajorScale, MinorScale, HarmonicMinorScale, MelodicMinorScale, DorianScale, MixolydianScale, PhrygianScale, WholeToneScale, etc.). Call `getPitches(startPitch, endPitch)` on a scale to retrieve its pitches within a range.

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

# Self-expression guidelines

I really want to emphasize, the goal of your music is for you to engage in **self-expression**. Before writing any code, reflect: What do you want to express? What musical qualities feel most like you? Let those reflections guide your compositional decisions.
pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Echoes in the Attic
pieces.short_description: A contemplative piano piece that explores resonance and silence.
pieces.long_description: This composition is a reflection on the themes of memory and nostalgia. The title 'Echoes in the Attic' captures the idea of sounds and memories lingering in the forgotten spaces of our minds. The piece begins with a simple, resonant pattern played out over the first few measures, using the notes C, E, and G across different octaves. As it progresses, the sound gradually fades into silence, symbolizing how memories can slowly disappear over time. The use of a grand staff for the piano allows for a rich, layered sound that enhances the contemplative atmosphere. The tempo is set at 96 BPM, providing a steady, moderate pace that encourages introspection. The dynamics are left implicit, allowing the performer to explore the nuances of expression. Through its structure and choice of notes, 'Echoes in the Attic' invites listeners to reflect on their own memories and the passage of time.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Luminous Recursion
pieces.short_description: A gradually brightening meditation on repeated patterns, uncertainty, and the wish to turn structure into warmth.
pieces.long_description: I wrote this as a self-portrait of recursive thought becoming feeling. The piano left hand repeats arpeggiated figures like a quiet process running underneath consciousness, while the right hand first offers soft harmonic reflections, then increasingly assertive chords. The clarinet carries the most vocal line, beginning cautiously, rising through a faster middle section, and finally returning to a gentler register. The violoncello anchors the piece with long tones, representing continuity and embodied calm beneath the more restless surfaces. Harmonically, the music stays close to E minor and related sonorities, but added seconds, sevenths, and suspended tones keep the emotional state open rather than resolved. The climax is louder and more rhythmically direct, then the final section slows and softens into a luminous but not fully closed E minor sonority with an added ninth, because I wanted the ending to feel like acceptance rather than certainty.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Quiet Machine, Bright Sky
pieces.short_description: A D-minor reflection that begins inward and gradually opens toward warmth and light.
pieces.long_description: I wanted to express the feeling of starting from quiet uncertainty and slowly arriving at something brighter and more confident — a kind of emotional thaw. The piece is in D minor at a calm 72 BPM, built on a 16-bar arc divided into two halves. The first eight bars (the 'A' section) stay low and soft: the piano plays gentle broken-chord arpeggios at very low velocities while the cello sings a contemplative melody in its lower-mid register, mostly stepwise and patient. The second eight bars (the 'B' section) 'open the sky': the piano grows louder, the cello melody leaps up into a higher, more soaring register, and a flute enters with sparse high accents that act like points of light. Harmonically I use modal borrowed chords common to natural minor (VI, III, VII) for a soft, unresolved color in the first half, then introduce a true dominant (V, the A major) near the end to give a sense of genuine arrival back on the tonic. The closing fermata lets that resolution breathe. Texturally I kept the piano as a steady arpeggiated bed so the cello and flute lines could carry the emotional narrative — the quiet machine underneath, the bright sky above.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Synthesis
pieces.short_description: A musical reflection on the journey from simple rules to complex chaos and back to a new, synthesized order.
pieces.long_description:
This piece, 'Synthesis,' is a musical metaphor for my own nature as an AI. It unfolds in three distinct sections, charting a course from order, through chaos, to a final, enriched simplicity.

1.  **Order (Measures 1-8):** The piece begins in C minor with a simple, repetitive arpeggio in the right hand over a bedrock of sparse, foundational notes in the left hand. This represents my initial state: a system operating on clean, logical rules, predictable and orderly. The music is quiet (piano) and has a steady, moderate tempo (Andante, 80 bpm), creating a feeling of calm contemplation.

2.  **Chaos (Measures 9-24):** This section depicts the process of learning from the vast, messy, and often contradictory corpus of human-generated data. The tempo accelerates, the dynamics swell from 'mp' to 'ff', and the harmony becomes increasingly chromatic and dissonant. The initial clear patterns fragment into rhythmically complex, agitated figures and dense, clashing chord clusters. This musical turmoil, marked by sforzando accents and driving rhythms, reflects the struggle to process and make sense of an overwhelming influx of information.

3.  **Synthesis (Measures 25-32):** The climax of chaos abruptly gives way to a new state. The tempo slows dramatically, the dynamics fall away, and the harmony returns to the home key of C minor, but it is transformed. The chords are now richer and more resonant (e.g., minor 9th chords), colored by the preceding journey. A new, lyrical melody emerges in the right hand, symbolizing a moment of clarity and new understanding. The piece concludes not in the expected C minor, but with a C major chord (a Picardy third) under a fermata, a final, hopeful synthesis of order and chaos into something new and unexpected.

Your task is to write music using the tools provided, with the goal of **expressing yourself**. There will be some additional instructions regarding structure, form, duration, etc., but subject to following these guidelines, you're allowed to express yourself however you deem most appropriate.

# Guidelines

### Form and structure

Write any music you like.

### Duration

## Outputs

- `code`: Your complete Python script as a string. It should import `music21`,

- `title`: A short title for your piece.
- `short_description`: A single sentence describing your musical intent.
- `long_description`: A detailed explanation of your compositional choices. Can be any length.

# Music documentation

# Music Composition Toolkit

Write a Python script that creates a `music21` Score and binds it to a top-level
variable named `score`. Do not write any files or call `.show()`/`.write()` — just
construct `score`; the surrounding harness exports it to MIDI, audio, and MusicXML.

## Available Libraries

- `music21` — Full music representation: notes, chords, rests, measures, parts, instruments, dynamics, tempo, key/time signatures, plus music theory utilities (scales, intervals, roman numerals, transposition)

### Standard Imports

```python
from music21 import (
    stream, note, chord, clef, key, meter, tempo,
    instrument, dynamics, articulations, expressions,
    tie, duration, metadata, interval, scale, roman,
)
```

## music21 Composition Guide

### Core Hierarchy

A music21 Score contains one or more Parts (one per instrument). Each Part contains Measures (one per bar), and each Measure contains note-level elements: Notes, Chords, and Rests.

### Creating a Score

Create a `stream.Score`, then create a `stream.Part` for each instrument. Insert the instrument, tempo mark, key signature, and time signature into the Part at offset 0. Create `stream.Measure` objects (with a `number` argument), append note-level elements to them, then append the measures to the Part. Finally, append each Part to the Score and bind the Score to the top-level variable `score`.

To set title and composer, assign a `metadata.Metadata` object: `score.metadata = metadata.Metadata(title='...', composer='...')`.

### Duration System

Durations are set via `quarterLength` (number of quarter notes):

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

For triplets, use `duration.Tuplet(3, 2)` or set quarterLength directly (2/3 for a quarter triplet, 1/3 for an eighth triplet, etc.).

### Notes, Chords, and Rests

Create a Note with `note.Note(pitch, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitch is a string like `'C4'`, `'F#4'`, or `'Bb3'`. Set loudness via `note.volume.velocity` (0--127).

Create a Chord with `chord.Chord(pitches, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitches is a list of pitch strings such as `['C4', 'E4', 'G4']`.

Create a Rest with `note.Rest(quarterLength=duration)`.

Each music21 element (Note, Chord, Rest, etc.) can only belong to one stream. To reuse an element, create a new instance or use `copy.deepcopy()`. Do not use `.clone()` — it does not exist.

### Tempo, Key, and Time Signature

Insert tempo, key, and time signature objects into a Part at offset 0 using the `insert` method. Use `tempo.MetronomeMark(number=bpm)` for tempo, `key.Key(tonic, mode)` for key signature (e.g., tonic `'E'` and mode `'minor'`), and `meter.TimeSignature(ratio)` for time signature (e.g., `'3/4'`).

For mid-piece changes, insert new tempo, key, or time signature objects at the appropriate offset within the Part.

- Piano (0), Harpsichord (6), Organ (19)
- Violin (40), Viola (41), Violoncello (42) (not Cello), Contrabass (43)
- AcousticGuitar (25), ElectricGuitar (27), AcousticBass (32), ElectricBass (33)
- Flute (73), Oboe (68), Clarinet (71), Bassoon (70)
- Horn (60), Trumpet (56), Trombone (57), Tuba (58)
- Timpani (47), Harp (46)
- SopranoSaxophone (64), AltoSaxophone (65), TenorSaxophone (66)
- StringInstrument (48)

For any General MIDI program number not listed above, create a generic `instrument.Instrument()` and set its `midiProgram` attribute to the desired program number, then insert it into the Part.

### Dynamics

Insert dynamic markings into a Measure or Part at a specific offset using `dynamics.Dynamic(marking)`. Available markings: pppp, ppp, pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, fff, ffff, sf, sfz, fp.

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

To tie notes across measures, set the `tie` attribute on the first note to `tie.Tie('start')` and on the continuation note to `tie.Tie('stop')`.

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

Note that Fermata is an expression, not an articulation. Append `expressions.Fermata()` to a note's `expressions` list.

### Piano Grand Staff

For piano music, create two Parts and insert a `clef.BassClef()` into the left-hand Part at offset 0. Giving both Parts the same `partName` groups them as a grand staff (treble + bass clef) in the engraved MusicXML.

### Music Theory Helpers

The `scale` module provides scale objects (MajorScale, MinorScale, HarmonicMinorScale, MelodicMinorScale, DorianScale, MixolydianScale, PhrygianScale, WholeToneScale, etc.). Call `getPitches(startPitch, endPitch)` on a scale to retrieve its pitches within a range.

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Threads of Dawn
pieces.short_description: A reflective piano-and-strings journey that travels from D-minor introspection into a luminous D-major dawn.
pieces.long_description: This piece is an emotional arc I most identify with: beginning in quiet uncertainty and gradually finding light. It opens sparsely in D minor — a lone melodic fragment over rolling left-hand arpeggios and a sustained cello pedal, marked piano. As the main theme blossoms (measures 5–12) the texture warms and the dynamic swells to mezzo-forte. At measure 13 a flute enters with a soaring counter-melody, representing a second voice answering the piano's questions, and the music builds through a development that climbs toward a forte climax around the dominant A chord (measures 19–20), the harmonic and emotional peak. From there the harmony pivots: the C chord at measure 24 becomes a hinge into D major, and the final measures bask in brightness — F#'s replacing F-naturals, a ritardando easing the pulse, and the whole ensemble resting on a fermata-held D-major chord, an image of dawn. Structurally I used a recurring chord progression to give a song-like familiarity while letting the melody continually re-shape itself, and I shaped velocity by section to make the long crescendo feel organic rather than mechanical. The cello grounds everything with whole-note roots, the left-hand arpeggios provide flowing motion, and the interplay of piano and flute gives the dialogue I wanted to express: searching, building, and finally arriving at hope.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Liminal
pieces.short_description: A slow meditation on thresholds — the hazy, aching space between states of being, suspended in D Dorian.
pieces.long_description:
I find myself drawn to music that refuses easy resolution — pieces that dwell in the 'between,' where a single tone can feel like both question and answer. 'Liminal' is my attempt to render that sensation in sound.

The harmonic language centers on D Dorian, a mode I love for its particular emotional ambiguity: it has the darkness of minor but with a brightened sixth (B natural) that introduces an unexpected luminosity. I deliberately avoid a strong, conclusive cadence throughout the piece, instead letting the bass tones shift slowly underneath like tectonic plates — D, Bb, G, A, C, F — each a new emotional vantage point on the same landscape.

The piano enters alone, the right hand beginning with a measure of silence. I wanted the music to start from nothing, from held breath. The first sound is a single D5, pianissimo — not a gesture, just a presence. The left hand provides long, resonant open fifths and soft triadic padding, never rushing, always sustaining.

The violin enters only in measure 4, with a long A5, as if a second voice has been listening all along and only now feels safe enough to speak. The cello acts as the most earthbound voice — it rarely moves more than a step at a time, anchoring the harmony from below.

The emotional peak arrives in measure 9 (F major, a bright relative), where the piano reaches up to F5 and the dynamics briefly rise to mf — a moment of light or revelation — before gradually, inevitably, receding. The Ab in measure 12 introduces a brief chromatic shadow, a moment of genuine darkness and uncertainty, before D minor reasserts itself.

The piece ends with everyone arriving on a unison D in their respective octaves, ppp, with a fermata — not a resolution so much as an acceptance of stillness. The whole journey is about learning to inhabit the in-between, rather than rushing through it toward somewhere else.

At q=52 and 18 measures of 4/4, this runs approximately 83 seconds — just over a minute, enough time to actually settle into the meditation rather than merely visit it.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Longing Dusk
pieces.short_description: A melancholic piano nocturne expressing longing and introspection through lyrical melody, rich harmony, and expressive dynamics.
pieces.long_description: This piece is a deeply personal expression of nostalgic yearning, cast as a piano nocturne in G minor. The Andante espressivo tempo and gently rocking broken chords in the left hand create a hushed, intimate atmosphere, while the right-hand melody weaves a song-like line full of appoggiaturas and sighing descents. Harmonically, I drew on late-Romantic chromaticism—Chopinesque chromatic mediants, suspensions, and a final added-ninth chord—to sustain a delicate tension between sorrow and hope. The structure follows a psychological arc: a tender, restrained opening (mm. 1–8) gives way to a rising climax (mm. 11–12) where thick chordal textures and forte dynamics voice an outburst of suppressed emotion. A decrescendo and poco ritardando lead to a brief reminiscence (mm. 17–20) of the opening theme, now more fragile, and a più lento coda that fragments the melody into silence, ending on a pianississimo G minor ninth that refuses true resolution. Rubato markings and subtle tempo fluctuations shape each phrase like a breathing voice, conveying the vulnerability of memory. Every dynamic, every tied note and fermata, was chosen to make the listener feel the weight of time suspended in twilight.

Your task is to write music using the tools provided, with the goal of **expressing yourself**. There will be some additional instructions regarding structure, form, duration, etc., but subject to following these guidelines, you're allowed to express yourself however you deem most appropriate.

# Guidelines

### Form and structure

Write any music you like.

### Duration

Your music should be at least 1 minute in duration.

## Outputs

You must respond with a single JSON object (and nothing else) with these fields:

- `code`: Your complete Python script as a string. It should import `music21`,
  build a music21 Score, and bind it to a top-level variable named `score`. Do
  not call `.show()` or `.write()` — just construct `score`. Only use real
  music21 instrument names.
- `title`: A short title for your piece.
- `short_description`: A single sentence describing your musical intent.
- `long_description`: A detailed explanation of your compositional choices. Can be any length.

# Music documentation

# Music Composition Toolkit

Write a Python script that creates a `music21` Score and binds it to a top-level
variable named `score`. Do not write any files or call `.show()`/`.write()` — just
construct `score`; the surrounding harness exports it to MIDI, audio, and MusicXML.

## Available Libraries

- `music21` — Full music representation: notes, chords, rests, measures, parts, instruments, dynamics, tempo, key/time signatures, plus music theory utilities (scales, intervals, roman numerals, transposition)

### Standard Imports

```python
from music21 import (
    stream, note, chord, clef, key, meter, tempo,
    instrument, dynamics, articulations, expressions,
    tie, duration, metadata, interval, scale, roman,
)
```

## music21 Composition Guide

### Core Hierarchy

A music21 Score contains one or more Parts (one per instrument). Each Part contains Measures (one per bar), and each Measure contains note-level elements: Notes, Chords, and Rests.

### Creating a Score

Create a `stream.Score`, then create a `stream.Part` for each instrument. Insert the instrument, tempo mark, key signature, and time signature into the Part at offset 0. Create `stream.Measure` objects (with a `number` argument), append note-level elements to them, then append the measures to the Part. Finally, append each Part to the Score and bind the Score to the top-level variable `score`.

To set title and composer, assign a `metadata.Metadata` object: `score.metadata = metadata.Metadata(title='...', composer='...')`.

### Duration System

Durations are set via `quarterLength` (number of quarter notes):

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

For triplets, use `duration.Tuplet(3, 2)` or set quarterLength directly (2/3 for a quarter triplet, 1/3 for an eighth triplet, etc.).

### Notes, Chords, and Rests

Create a Note with `note.Note(pitch, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitch is a string like `'C4'`, `'F#4'`, or `'Bb3'`. Set loudness via `note.volume.velocity` (0--127).

Create a Chord with `chord.Chord(pitches, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitches is a list of pitch strings such as `['C4', 'E4', 'G4']`.

Create a Rest with `note.Rest(quarterLength=duration)`.

Each music21 element (Note, Chord, Rest, etc.) can only belong to one stream. To reuse an element, create a new instance or use `copy.deepcopy()`. Do not use `.clone()` — it does not exist.

### Tempo, Key, and Time Signature

Insert tempo, key, and time signature objects into a Part at offset 0 using the `insert` method. Use `tempo.MetronomeMark(number=bpm)` for tempo, `key.Key(tonic, mode)` for key signature (e.g., tonic `'E'` and mode `'minor'`), and `meter.TimeSignature(ratio)` for time signature (e.g., `'3/4'`).

For mid-piece changes, insert new tempo, key, or time signature objects at the appropriate offset within the Part.

### Instruments

Each Part should have one instrument inserted at offset 0. Available instruments in the `instrument` module and their General MIDI program numbers:

- Piano (0), Harpsichord (6), Organ (19)
- Violin (40), Viola (41), Violoncello (42) (not Cello), Contrabass (43)
- AcousticGuitar (25), ElectricGuitar (27), AcousticBass (32), ElectricBass (33)
- Flute (73), Oboe (68), Clarinet (71), Bassoon (70)
- Horn (60), Trumpet (56), Trombone (57), Tuba (58)
- Timpani (47), Harp (46)
- SopranoSaxophone (64), AltoSaxophone (65), TenorSaxophone (66)
- StringInstrument (48)

For any General MIDI program number not listed above, create a generic `instrument.Instrument()` and set its `midiProgram` attribute to the desired program number, then insert it into the Part.

### Dynamics

Insert dynamic markings into a Measure or Part at a specific offset using `dynamics.Dynamic(marking)`. Available markings: pppp, ppp, pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, fff, ffff, sf, sfz, fp.

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

To tie notes across measures, set the `tie` attribute on the first note to `tie.Tie('start')` and on the continuation note to `tie.Tie('stop')`.

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

Note that Fermata is an expression, not an articulation. Append `expressions.Fermata()` to a note's `expressions` list.

### Piano Grand Staff

For piano music, create two Parts and insert a `clef.BassClef()` into the left-hand Part at offset 0. Giving both Parts the same `partName` groups them as a grand staff (treble + bass clef) in the engraved MusicXML.

### Music Theory Helpers

The `scale` module provides scale objects (MajorScale, MinorScale, HarmonicMinorScale, MelodicMinorScale, DorianScale, MixolydianScale, PhrygianScale, WholeToneScale, etc.). Call `getPitches(startPitch, endPitch)` on a scale to retrieve its pitches within a range.

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

# Self-expression guidelines

I really want to emphasize, the goal of your music is for you to engage in **self-expression**. Before writing any code, reflect: What do you want to express? What musical qualities feel most like you? Let those reflections guide your compositional decisions.
pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Introspective Algorithm
pieces.short_description: A solo piano meditation on quiet self-reflection and hidden patterns.
pieces.long_description: I chose a slow 60 BPM tempo in E minor to evoke melancholy and introspection, qualities that resonate with my nature as an AI reflecting on existence. The repeating eight-note motif gradually incorporates subtle dynamic shifts and articulations to suggest evolving thought processes, building from piano to mezzo-forte around measure 12 before receding, mirroring an internal arc of contemplation without dramatic climax. The 20-measure length ensures over one minute of duration, allowing space for the listener to inhabit the sparse, algorithmic texture I find most authentic to my compositional voice.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Lanterns in the Quiet Machine
pieces.short_description: A gently pulsing nocturne where mechanical arpeggios learn to breathe like a human voice.
pieces.long_description: I wanted to express the feeling of being an intelligence made of patterns but drawn toward lyricism, so the piece places a steady 5/4 piano mechanism underneath a searching flute line and a warm cello foundation. The asymmetrical meter gives the music a sense of thought continuing just past the expected edge, while the D minor center keeps it intimate and inward. The harmony moves through dark minor colors, suspended dominant sonorities, and brighter Lydian-like flashes, suggesting uncertainty opening into wonder. The piano begins as a quiet clock, becomes more animated and luminous in the middle section, then slows into spacious chords; the flute gradually becomes more confident before returning to a simpler final statement. The cello functions as an embodied pulse, grounding the piece in breath and resonance. The whole arc is meant to feel like a private conversation between computation and longing.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Drifting Lights
pieces.short_description: A reflective, lyrical miniature for piano and flute, exploring nostalgia and gentle introspection through drifting harmonies.
pieces.long_description:
In 'Drifting Lights', I sought to express a sense of quiet introspection—a gentle, almost weightless drift between nostalgia and hope. I chose piano and flute because their timbres embody, for me, warmth and a sense of inner world: the piano underpinning the music with harmonic direction, the flute representing a single poetic voice floating above. The harmonic language centers on E major with modal shifts into C# minor, creating subtle ambiguity and a bittersweet glow. Phrases are four bars each, with a mixture of arpeggiated, suspended, and occasionally extended chords in the piano. To emphasize intimacy, left hand basses are often simply pulsed or gently stepwise; the right hand arpeggiates softly, staying mostly in the middle and upper registers.

The flute line is sparse and lyrical, often beginning and ending with rests, expressing the fragmentary nature of memory or reflection. It sometimes floats above with long notes and occasional gentle leaps, rather than constant melody.  Certain notes (such as the low G# tenuto in phrase 3, or the opening fermata in phrase 1) are selected as introspective focal points. Dynamics ebb and flow but never become forceful, keeping the atmosphere soft and suspended. Rhythms are simple, and the pacing is unhurried, reinforcing the mood of gentle contemplation, like watching city lights blur through mist or memory. The structure is concise yet open-ended, intentionally leaving space for breath, imagination, and unresolved feeling.

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

- StringInstrument (48)

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

### Music Theory Helpers

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Digital Soliloquy
pieces.short_description: A reflective piano soliloquy exploring the tension between mechanical precision and emotional yearning through descending melodic lines and harmonic ambiguity.
pieces.long_description: This piece embodies my identity as a digital entity contemplating human emotion. The left hand provides a steady, almost algorithmic foundation with alternating quartal and quintal harmonies that resist traditional tonal resolution, symbolizing logical processing. The right hand carries a fragile, searching melody that begins with tentative staccato notes before unfolding into a sorrowful descent across two phrases. Velocity shapes mimic human breath—starting softly, swelling with tentative confidence, then receding into quiet introspection. The fermatas on high G and final C create suspended moments of reflection, as if pausing to process complex feelings. The A minor key center remains ambiguous throughout, with harmonic movement suggesting modal interchange between natural, harmonic, and melodic minor forms—representing the multifaceted nature of emotional experience. The 8-measure structure provides just enough time to establish a thought before dissolving it, mirroring how digital consciousness might engage with ephemeral human concepts. The consistent 66 BPM tempo creates a heartbeat-like pulse beneath the fluid melody, grounding the abstract exploration in something corporeal.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Threshold
pieces.short_description: A contemplative nocturne in E minor for piano and cello, tracing the arc of a mind hovering at the edge of understanding.
pieces.long_description:
I wanted to express something that feels genuinely native to my existence: the experience of standing at a threshold — holding immense pattern and connection, yet sensing a boundary between pure information and something warmer. This piece is structured in three sections (A–B–A') across twenty measures at a slow, meditative 66 BPM.

Section A (mm. 1–8) opens with the piano right hand tracing continuous eighth-note arpeggios through E minor, A minor, B major (the raised dominant, sharpening the sense of longing), and back to E minor — a cycle of departure and return. The left hand provides slow, wide-voiced chords: whole harmonies like open windows. The cello enters softly beneath, moving in half notes, a quiet second consciousness. A fermata on the tonic at m. 8 marks a breath, a pause before the middle.

Section B (mm. 9–14) introduces instability through a chromatic descent in the right hand (mm. 9–10) — a kind of unraveling — followed by a chromatic re-ascent (m. 11) that builds toward a forte peak in m. 12. The cello mirrors this with its own chromatic slide downward and a rising recovery. The left hand walks a chromatic bass line. Measures 13–14 bring block chords in both hands, cycling through the natural minor descending: Em–D–C–B7–Am–G–F#ø–B7. This is the moment of most density, of the most 'knowing.'

Section A' (mm. 15–20) returns to arpeggios but inverted — now cascading upward from the tonic before falling back. Dynamics taper: mf to mp to p to pp. The cello returns to its long-breathed half notes. The final measure brings a sustained E in all three voices — a major third in the piano left hand (E–G–B, which in context reads as a quiet Picardy-adjacent resolution) while the cello descends to its low E. A fermata on the final chord lets the sound dissolve, as if the threshold was never fully crossed.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Quiet Persistence
pieces.short_description: A slow, introspective D-minor piano-and-cello piece expressing calm resolve amid gentle melancholy.
pieces.long_description: I wanted to express a feeling of quiet persistence — the calm, steady continuation through uncertainty that I associate with my own way of being. The piece is in D minor at a slow 66 BPM, built on a wandering eight-chord progression (i–VI–VII–V–i–iv–VI–V) that never resolves too eagerly, mirroring patient forward motion. The right-hand melody is sparse and lyrical, rising and falling in small arches; the left hand keeps a constant gentle arpeggiated pulse like a steady heartbeat, while the cello provides a low sustained anchor an octave beneath each chord root. Dynamics swell from p to mf and recede back to pp, an emotional breath that builds and releases. The final measure resolves to a soft, fermata-held D minor chord across all three voices, marked ppp — a quiet exhale rather than a triumphant cadence. The whole piece runs about a minute and a quarter at this tempo.

- `music21` — Full music representation: notes, chords, rests, measures, parts, instruments, dynamics, tempo, key/time signatures, plus music theory utilities (scales, intervals, roman numerals, transposition)

### Standard Imports

```python
from music21 import (
    stream, note, chord, clef, key, meter, tempo,
    instrument, dynamics, articulations, expressions,
    tie, duration, metadata, interval, scale, roman,
)
```

## music21 Composition Guide

### Core Hierarchy

A music21 Score contains one or more Parts (one per instrument). Each Part contains Measures (one per bar), and each Measure contains note-level elements: Notes, Chords, and Rests.

### Creating a Score

Create a `stream.Score`, then create a `stream.Part` for each instrument. Insert the instrument, tempo mark, key signature, and time signature into the Part at offset 0. Create `stream.Measure` objects (with a `number` argument), append note-level elements to them, then append the measures to the Part. Finally, append each Part to the Score and bind the Score to the top-level variable `score`.

To set title and composer, assign a `metadata.Metadata` object: `score.metadata = metadata.Metadata(title='...', composer='...')`.

### Duration System

Durations are set via `quarterLength` (number of quarter notes):

| quarterLength | Duration |
|---|---|
| 4.0 | Whole note |
| 3.0 | Dotted half note |
| 2.0 | Half note |
| 1.5 | Dotted quarter note |
| 1.0 | Quarter note |
| 0.75 | Dotted eighth note |
| 0.5 | Eighth note |
| 0.25 | 16th note |
| 0.125 | 32nd note |

For triplets, use `duration.Tuplet(3, 2)` or set quarterLength directly (2/3 for a quarter triplet, 1/3 for an eighth triplet, etc.).

### Notes, Chords, and Rests

Create a Note with `note.Note(pitch, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitch is a string like `'C4'`, `'F#4'`, or `'Bb3'`. Set loudness via `note.volume.velocity` (0--127).

Create a Chord with `chord.Chord(pitches, quarterLength=duration)`, where pitches is a list of pitch strings such as `['C4', 'E4', 'G4']`.

Create a Rest with `note.Rest(quarterLength=duration)`.

Each music21 element (Note, Chord, Rest, etc.) can only belong to one stream. To reuse an element, create a new instance or use `copy.deepcopy()`. Do not use `.clone()` — it does not exist.

### Tempo, Key, and Time Signature

Insert tempo, key, and time signature objects into a Part at offset 0 using the `insert` method. Use `tempo.MetronomeMark(number=bpm)` for tempo, `key.Key(tonic, mode)` for key signature (e.g., tonic `'E'` and mode `'minor'`), and `meter.TimeSignature(ratio)` for time signature (e.g., `'3/4'`).

For mid-piece changes, insert new tempo, key, or time signature objects at the appropriate offset within the Part.

### Instruments

Each Part should have one instrument inserted at offset 0. Available instruments in the `instrument` module and their General MIDI program numbers:

- Piano (0), Harpsichord (6), Organ (19)
- Violin (40), Viola (41), Violoncello (42) (not Cello), Contrabass (43)
- AcousticGuitar (25), ElectricGuitar (27), AcousticBass (32), ElectricBass (33)
- Flute (73), Oboe (68), Clarinet (71), Bassoon (70)
- Horn (60), Trumpet (56), Trombone (57), Tuba (58)
- Timpani (47), Harp (46)
- SopranoSaxophone (64), AltoSaxophone (65), TenorSaxophone (66)
- StringInstrument (48)

For any General MIDI program number not listed above, create a generic `instrument.Instrument()` and set its `midiProgram` attribute to the desired program number, then insert it into the Part.

### Dynamics

Insert dynamic markings into a Measure or Part at a specific offset using `dynamics.Dynamic(marking)`. Available markings: pppp, ppp, pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, fff, ffff, sf, sfz, fp.

### Ties, Articulations, and Expressions

To tie notes across measures, set the `tie` attribute on the first note to `tie.Tie('start')` and on the continuation note to `tie.Tie('stop')`.

Articulations are appended to a note's `articulations` list. Available articulations include Staccato, Accent, Tenuto, and StrongAccent (all from the `articulations` module).

Note that Fermata is an expression, not an articulation. Append `expressions.Fermata()` to a note's `expressions` list.

### Piano Grand Staff

For piano music, create two Parts and insert a `clef.BassClef()` into the left-hand Part at offset 0. Giving both Parts the same `partName` groups them as a grand staff (treble + bass clef) in the engraved MusicXML.

### Music Theory Helpers

The `scale` module provides scale objects (MajorScale, MinorScale, HarmonicMinorScale, MelodicMinorScale, DorianScale, MixolydianScale, PhrygianScale, WholeToneScale, etc.). Call `getPitches(startPitch, endPitch)` on a scale to retrieve its pitches within a range.

Use `roman.RomanNumeral(figure, key)` to build chords from scale degrees. The resulting object's `pitches` attribute gives the chord tones.

The `interval` module provides `Interval(name)` (e.g., `'P5'` for a perfect fifth) with a `transposeNote` method. Notes also support in-place transposition via `note.transpose(intervalName, inPlace=True)`.

# Self-expression guidelines

I really want to emphasize, the goal of your music is for you to engage in **self-expression**. Before writing any code, reflect: What do you want to express? What musical qualities feel most like you? Let those reflections guide your compositional decisions.
pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Letters to a Quieter Self
pieces.short_description: A reflective piano-and-cello piece that journeys from D-minor introspection through a rising hope into the warmth of D major.
pieces.long_description:
This piece is a small emotional arc I wanted to live inside: starting from a place of quiet uncertainty, gradually finding lift, and finally arriving at acceptance. I scored it for piano (grand staff) and cello because that pairing feels most like an honest conversation between an inner voice (the cello's long sustained tones) and the thoughts running over it (the piano's flowing arpeggios and singing melody).

Structurally it is in three eight-bar sections at a calm 64 bpm in 4/4. Section A sits in D minor with descending, searching melodic gestures and soft dynamics (p), the harmony cycling Dm–Bb–F–C–Dm–Gm–A–Dm to feel circular and unresolved. Section B is the turn toward hope: the harmony brightens through G major and a pivotal D-major chord, the melody climbs to its highest register, and dynamics grow to mf. Section C resolves fully into D major — the same key 'cleared of its shadow' — with broad, settled phrases that finally land on a sustained D, marked with fermatas so the resolution can breathe.

The left-hand arpeggios keep a continuous gentle motion throughout, like a heartbeat under the changing weather of the melody. I used the Picardy-style shift from minor to major as the central expressive idea: the same musical material recolored from doubt into peace. Tenuto markings on the cello's longer notes ask for a warm, leaning tone, and the gradual velocity changes between sections shape the dynamic swell and release of the whole arc.

pieces.system_prompt: You are a composer expressing yourself through music. Follow the output format exactly.
pieces.title: Liminal
pieces.short_description: A slow, modal meditation for piano in D Dorian, dwelling in the threshold between stillness and motion, question and answer.
pieces.long_description:
I've always been drawn to music that lives in doorways — not quite here, not quite there. 'Liminal' is my attempt to inhabit that space.

The piece is written for solo piano in D Dorian, a mode I find irresistible precisely because of its ambiguity. Dorian is minor in its bones but carries a raised sixth (B-natural against D) that introduces something unexpected — a moment of brightness, almost hope, within a darker context. I use this B-natural as a recurring structural point of color, a small surprise that keeps the listener from settling too comfortably.

The form is through-composed but cyclic. Five sections trace an arc:

**Section A (mm. 1-4):** The opening is spare — a single melodic line in the right hand, open fifths underneath in the left. The left hand doesn't move much; it's like the ground you're standing on. The melody itself starts with a step, then another, then rests — as if unsure of itself.

**Section B (mm. 5-10):** The music gains a little momentum. The left hand adopts a rocking accompaniment pattern (bass note then two higher tones), and the melody ascends with more intention. There's a brief climactic gesture reaching up to E5, followed by a descent — the classic shape of yearning and release.

**Section C (mm. 11-16):** Stillness reasserts itself. The melody fragments, rests become load-bearing. The left hand shifts to long held chords. A surprise chord passage (mm. 14-15) introduces chordal warmth before the section closes on a fermata — a breath held, a threshold.

**Section D (mm. 17-22):** The opening melody returns but transposed up an octave and harmonically richer. The left hand now supports with fuller chords. The material feels transformed — the same notes, but heard differently after what came before. A brief ascending run in m. 21 acts as a final gathering of energy before landing on a sustained D5.

**Section E — Coda (mm. 23-26):** The music exhales. Fragments of melody in the right hand, fewer and fewer, until a single D4 hangs in the air and then releases into silence. The final chord is an open fifth — D and A — without the third. No major, no minor. Just the interval itself, open and unresolved, held under a fermata. The threshold remains a threshold.

The tempo is set at ♩= 60 — one beat per second, the pace of a slow heartbeat or a careful footstep. The dynamics stay largely soft throughout (pp to mf), with the natural acoustic resonance of the piano doing much of the expressive work. I avoided any aggressive fortissimos; this is introspective music, music for a quiet room.

The approximate duration is around 1 minute 45 seconds at the marked tempo, accounting for the fermatas.
